methods.description: This method describes the setup and conditions for observing the foraging behavior of Bombus terrestris in a controlled outdoor environment. The purpose is to study the foraging patterns and resource collection preferences of bumble bees.

steps.description: All tests were conducted in an outdoor flight cage, consisting of an aluminium frame covered with net fabric, in the Botanical Garden of the Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf, Germany.
steps.searchable_summary: set up outdoor flight cage for bee observation
inputs: location: Botanical Garden of the Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf

steps.description: A colony of Bombus terrestris (Biofa AG, Münsingen, Germany) was maintained in this flight cage and provided with diverse flowering plants from various families.
steps.searchable_summary: maintain Bombus terrestris colony with diverse plants

steps.description: A feeder containing a 30% sugar solution provided a carbohydrate supply to the colony.
steps.searchable_summary: provide sugar solution feeder for bees

steps.description: To motivate bees to collect pollen from the plants in the flight cage, supplemental pollen was not provided.
steps.searchable_summary: omit supplemental pollen to encourage natural foraging
inputs: decision to not provide supplemental pollen

steps.description: Most bumble bees collected both nectar and pollen, but during one foraging bout, they settled on one resource.
steps.searchable_summary: observe resource selection during foraging bouts

steps.description: During a given bout, foragers either visited flowers to collect nectar or they collected mainly pollen and also some nectar to pack the pollen grains in their corbiculae.
steps.searchable_summary: record foraging task based on resource collection

steps.description: According to their bout-based foraging task, we defined the bumble bees as nectar and pollen foragers for individual trials.
steps.searchable_summary: classify bees as nectar or pollen foragers
